title_en: Theological Methodology and the Qadariyyah Connection
content_en:
The title of Ibn Taymiyyah's work pairs two groups: the Shia and the Qadariyyah. This pairing is not incidental but reflects a methodological observation that runs throughout the work. The Qadariyyah — broadly, those who denied divine predetermination and asserted that human beings create their own actions independently of divine decree — represented for Ibn Taymiyyah a shared error of method with certain Shia theological schools, even though the substantive doctrines were different.

The shared error was an excessive reliance on a particular kind of rational argument derived from kalam philosophy to override transmitted religious content. The Mu'tazila, who were closely associated with Qadari theology, had argued that divine justice required that God not predetermine evil actions and then punish humans for them. This rational argument about divine justice led them to reinterpret or reject prophetic hadiths that clearly affirmed divine predetermination. A parallel error appeared in Shia theology: rational arguments about what a just and wise God would have done — designating an infallible Imam, ensuring the succession was not usurped — led to readings of history and scripture that Ibn Taymiyyah considered unsupported by the actual evidence.

In this section of the work, Ibn Taymiyyah develops a detailed analysis of how kalam methodology functions when applied to questions of theology and history. He distinguishes between different types of claims — those that can be established by transmitted evidence, those that require rational inference, and those that are accessible only through revelation. His argument is that both the Shia and Qadari errors arise from importing the wrong type of argument into domains where a different type is appropriate.

For questions of history — who said what, what events occurred, how the companions conducted themselves — the appropriate instrument is careful examination of transmitted accounts with attention to chain of transmission and consistency with other reliable evidence. For questions of theology — the nature of God, the reality of divine decree, the meaning of divine justice — the appropriate starting point is Quranic and prophetic teaching, with rational inquiry playing a supporting rather than overriding role.

Ibn Taymiyyah also discusses the relationship between theological methodology and sectarian identity. He observes that groups which prioritized a particular rational framework tended to read scripture and history through the lens of that framework, finding confirmation everywhere and dismissing contradictory evidence. This confirmation bias reinforced group identity and made genuine engagement with contrary evidence difficult. He sees this dynamic at work in both the Shia and Qadari traditions of his time.

This analysis connects to Ibn Taymiyyah's broader project in Dar' Ta'arud al-Aql wan-Naql, making Minhaj as-Sunnah a practical application of the epistemological principles developed in that larger work. The question of how reason and transmitted knowledge should relate is not merely abstract; it determined how entire communities read their history, understood their obligations, and organized their religious lives.
